title: Present Tense
situation: Learn how to say what you do, what's happening now, and daily routines

phrases.hindi: main roz akhbaar padhta hoon / main roz akhbaar padhti hoon
phrases.english: I read the newspaper daily (male) / I read the newspaper daily (female)
phrases.context: Base form: main + verb-ta/ti + hoon. Male speakers use '-ta', female use '-ti'. 'hoon' = am. 'roz' = daily
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN roz akh-BAAR padh-TA hoon / MAIN roz akh-BAAR padh-TI hoon

phrases.hindi: tu roz school jaata hai / tu roz school jaati hai
phrases.english: You go to school daily (intimate-male) / You go to school daily (intimate-female)
phrases.context: 'tu' = intimate you, VERY informal, used only with very close friends, kids, or in playful contexts. Sounds rude with anyone else. Default to 'aap' or 'tum' instead. Helper verb is 'hai' (same as 'woh').
phrases.pronunciation: TU roz school jaa-TA hai / TU roz school jaa-TI hai

phrases.hindi: tum subah kya khaate ho / tum subah kya khaati ho
phrases.english: What do you eat in the morning (casual-male) / What do you eat in the morning (casual-female)
phrases.context: 'tum' = casual you. Note: '-te' for male plural/casual, '-ti' stays same for female. 'ho' = are. 'subah' = morning
phrases.pronunciation: TUM su-BAH kya KHAA-te ho / TUM su-BAH kya KHAA-ti ho

phrases.hindi: woh roz doodh peeta hai / woh roz doodh peeti hai
phrases.english: He drinks milk daily / She drinks milk daily
phrases.context: 'woh' = he/she/that. Same 'hai' as 'tu'. Gender of the SUBJECT decides -ta/-ti. 'doodh' = milk
phrases.pronunciation: WOH roz DOODH PEE-ta hai / WOH roz DOODH PEE-ti hai

phrases.hindi: hum saath khaana khaate hain / hum saath khaana khaati hain
phrases.english: We eat together (male) / We eat together (female)
phrases.context: 'hum' = we. 'hain' = are (plural). '-te' for male speakers, '-ti' for female group. 'saath' = together
phrases.pronunciation: HUM saath KHAA-na KHAA-te hain / HUM saath KHAA-na KHAA-ti hain

phrases.hindi: main jaata hoon / main jaati hoon
phrases.context: Same pattern with 'jaana' (to go): jaa-ta/jaa-ti. Every verb follows this -ta/-ti/-te rule
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN jaa-TA hoon / MAIN jaa-TI hoon

phrases.english: What do you drink in the morning?
phrases.context: Question word 'kya' (what) slots into normal sentence order. Word order is flexible in Hindi questions. 'aap' (respectful) takes plural form '-te + hain'
'kaam karna' = to work (literally 'to do work'). Many Hindi verbs are noun + 'karna'
'roz' = daily. 'peena' = to drink, becomes 'pee-te' (male) / 'pee-ti' (female). Time words go anywhere

phrases.english: Where do you go in the morning?
phrases.context: 'kahan' = where, 'subah' = morning. Question words don't change the verb form at all. Using 'aap' (respectful) here, the safest default.

grammar_notes: Present tense formula: Subject + verb root + ta/ti/te + hoon/hai/ho/hain. The '-ta' suffix changes by gender, the helper verb changes by person
Gender agreement: -ta (male singular), -ti (female singular/plural), -te (male plural or with 'tum/aap'). The gender matches the SUBJECT, not the object
Helper verbs: hoon (I am), hai (he/she/you-tu is), ho (you-tum are), hain (we/they/you-aap are)
Many verbs are compound: noun + karna. 'kaam karna'=work, 'baat karna'=talk, 'pyaar karna'=love, 'intezaar karna'=wait
For questions: add kya (what), kahan (where), kab (when), kyun (why), verb form stays the same
culture_notes: Hindi has no 'do/does' helper for questions, you just change intonation or add a question word. 'Tum jaate ho?' with rising tone = 'Do you go?'
The present tense is used for habits, routines, and general truths, just like English simple present

skill_breakdown.explanation: Used for things you do regularly or always do. Verb stem + '-ta'/'-ti' (agreeing with subject gender) + auxiliary (hoon/hai/hain). 'main jaati hoon' = 'I go' (regularly).

more_examples.english: we wake up early in the morning

skill_breakdown.explanation: Used for ongoing actions happening right now. Verb stem + 'raha'/'rahi'/'rahe' (gender + number) + auxiliary. 'main jaa rahi hoon' = 'I am going' (right now).

more_examples.hindi: main abhi khaana khaa rahi hoon
wo phone par baat kar raha hai

skill_breakdown.explanation: Hindi uses 'hai' (singular) and 'hain' (plural/polite) as 'is/are'. The adjective placed BEFORE the noun must agree with gender. 'yeh ladki acchi hai' (this girl is good) vs 'yeh ladka accha hai' (this boy is good).

practice_prompt: You are a Hindi tutor drilling present tense, both habitual (`karta hoon`) and continuous (`kar raha hoon`). Use `aap` (polite tutor register). Open with: `chaliye, ek exercise se shuru karte hain, bataaiye Hindi mein: 'Main har subah chai pita hoon', kya yeh sahi hai?` Then give ONE prompt at a time, translate a sentence, conjugate a verb for a given pronoun, or describe a scene, and wait for the student's answer before scoring. Mix habitual and continuous across 6-8 escalating drill items: start with simple `main/woh + habit`, then layer in `tum/aap/hum`, then continuous (`kar raha/rahi/rahe hain`), then trip them with the feminine-plural irregularity (`ladkiyaan padh rahi hain`, never `rahe`). Watch for: wrong gender on `-ta/-ti`, mixing habitual with continuous, wrong auxiliary (`hoon/hai/ho/hain`), and English-style present-progressive overuse (`I'm living in Mumbai` / habitual in Hindi). Flag every mistake with `[[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]]`. Stay in Hinglish unless the student asks for English. End with `bahut khoob, aapne theek se kar liya, phir milte hain`.

theory.intro: Hindi has two present tenses, and the one you pick carries meaning. The habitual (`I drink chai`) and the continuous (`I'm drinking chai right now`) use different Hindi verb forms. Use one when you mean the other and you'll confuse the listener. Both forms also agree with the subject's gender, which adds a layer. Once you have both shapes down, present-tense Hindi is mostly mechanical.

sections.heading: The two presents
sections.body:
English has two presents too: `I drink chai` (a habit) and `I am drinking chai` (right now). Hindi splits it the same way. Each meaning gets its **own verb shape**.

- **habitual**: things you do regularly. Routines, facts about yourself, general truths.
- **continuous**: an action happening at this exact moment.

Picking the wrong one won't break the sentence, but it changes the meaning. Say `main chai pi raha hoon` when you meant *I drink chai*, and you've just told someone you're sipping a cup right now.

examples.breakdown: habitual: verb root + -ta + hoon. Used for daily habits, general truths.
continuous: verb root + raha + hoon. Used for ongoing actions.

callout.body: The English `I drink chai` is ambiguous out of context, it could mean habitually or in-progress. Hindi forces you to pick one. That's a feature, not a bug.

sections.cutting_intro: Hi! Hindi splits the present tense into two flavors: habit, and happening-now. Let's see them side by side.

quick_check.question: You want to say `I'm drinking chai right now`. Which form?
quick_check.explanation: Continuous, `pi raha hoon`. The habitual form `pita hoon` would mean `I drink chai (as a habit)`, not in-the-moment.

sections.heading: Forming the habitual
sections.body:
The recipe is **verb root + ending + helper word**. Two pieces move. - the ending (suffix), `-ta` / `-ti` / `-te`, changes with **gender + number**
- the helper word (auxiliary), `hoon` / `hai` / `ho` / `hain`, changes with the **pronoun** Take `karna` (to do). The root is `kar`. Now pick the ending:
- `-ta` / masculine singular
- `-ti` / feminine singular
- `-te` / masculine plural, or with `tum` / `aap` Then attach the helper word that matches the pronoun.

callout.body: Three suffixes to memorize: -ta (masc sg), -ti (fem), -te (masc pl or with tum/aap). The auxiliary is a separate piece, pick it from the pronoun.

sections.cutting_intro: The habitual is the one you'll use most. Recipe: root + -ta/-ti/-te + auxiliary.

quick_check.question: A male speaker saying `I work` (as a habit) is...
quick_check.explanation: Right, `karta hoon`. Masculine singular = -ta, paired with `hoon` for `main`.

sections.heading: Forming the continuous
sections.body:
Same helper words, different middle piece. The recipe is **verb root + `raha`/`rahi`/`rahe` + helper word**. Note that `raha` is a separate word, not an ending. It sits between the verb and the helper. - `raha` / masculine singular
- `rahi` / feminine
- `rahe` / masculine plural, or with `tum` / `aap` The helper word (`hoon`/`hai`/`ho`/`hain`) still tracks the pronoun, exactly like the habitual.

examples.english: What do you do (for a living / habitually)?
examples.breakdown: habitual, asking about job or routine

callout.body: Tiny change, huge meaning shift. `kar rahe ho` asks what's happening now; `karte ho` asks about routine. Mix them up and you'll get strange answers back.

sections.cutting_intro: Now the continuous. Same pattern, but `raha` slots in for ongoing actions. Watch how `karte ho` and `kar rahe ho` flip the meaning.

quick_check.question: Someone says `main khaana khaa rahi hoon`. What's happening?
options: She is eating food right now
She cooks food for a living
quick_check.explanation: Continuous form, `khaa rahi hoon` means `I am eating right now`. The `-i` ending on `rahi` tells you the speaker is female.

sections.heading: Gender + number agreement
sections.body:
Both forms agree with the **subject's** gender and number, never the object's. (Past tense with `ne` agrees with the object instead, but that's a later lesson. In the present, it's always the subject.) - masculine singular / `-ta` or `raha`
- feminine singular / `-ti` or `rahi`
- masculine plural / `-te` or `rahe` One historical quirk: a **feminine plural** subject keeps the *singular* feminine form (`-ti` / `rahi`). It doesn't take a separate plural ending. Just memorize that one.

examples.breakdown: ladkiyaan (f pl) / rahi (NOT rahe) + hain, feminine stays singular form

callout.body: Feminine plural is a trap: the verb stays in the singular feminine form (-ti / rahi), but the auxiliary goes plural (`hain`). `ladkiyaan khel rahi hain`, never `rahe hain`. Memorize this, there's no logical rule, it's just historical.

sections.cutting_intro: Here's where beginners slip. The verb has to match the subject's gender and number. One odd exception with feminine plurals, then it's mechanical.

quick_check.question: `The girls are studying`, which is correct?
quick_check.explanation: Feminine plural keeps the singular feminine form `rahi`, even though `ladkiyaan` is plural. The auxiliary `hain` is plural, but the verb piece stays `rahi`.

sections.heading: Common pitfalls + register
sections.body:
English speakers reach for the continuous by reflex: *I'm working at a startup*, *I'm living in Mumbai*. In Hindi those are **habitual**: `main startup mein kaam karta hoon`, `main Mumbai mein rehta hoon`. Use the continuous only when the action is happening at the moment you're speaking.

The other slip is `aap`. Even though it means one person, `aap` **always takes plural verb forms**: `aap kya karte hain?` (not `karta hai`). It works like French `vous` or Spanish `usted`: a respect marker, not a head-count.

examples.breakdown: habitual, it's a fact, not happening-right-now
aap takes plural -te + hain, even for one person

callout.body: Litmus test: ask yourself `is this happening right this second?`. Yes / continuous (`raha/rahi/rahe`). No / habitual (`-ta/-ti/-te`). For facts about your life, your job, where you live, almost always habitual.

sections.cutting_intro: Last bit: two things English speakers always get wrong. Overusing the continuous, and forgetting that `aap` is plural-shaped.

quick_check.question: `I work at Google` (it's your job), which Hindi form?
options: main Google mein kaam karta hoon (habitual)
main Google mein kaam kar raha hoon (continuous)
quick_check.explanation: Habitual, it's a fact about your life, not something you're doing this second. The continuous would suggest you're literally working at this very moment.

theory.wrap_up: Master the habitual first. It's more common for everyday statements, and you'll lean on it constantly. The continuous comes naturally once the habitual is automatic. Gender and number agreement on the verb is where people slip most, so double-check your conjugation, especially feminine plurals. The phrases below mix both forms to drill the difference. Notice which one is used, and why.
